title: End-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform
optimized_headline: Revolutionary Optical Platform Enables Fully Autonomous Scientific Discoveries

raw_body:
arXiv:2604.27092v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Scientific research has long been human-led, driving new knowledge and transformative technologies through the continual revision of questions, methods and claims as evidence accumulates. Although large language model (LLM)-based agents are beginning to move beyond assisting predefined research workflows, none has yet demonstrated end-to-end autonomous discovery in a real physical system that produces a nontrivial result supported by experimental evidence. Here we introduce Qiushi Discovery Engine, an LLM-based agentic system for end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform. Qiushi Engine combines nonlinear research phases, Meta-Trace memory and a dual-layer architecture to maintain adaptive and stable research trajectories across long-horizon investigations involving thousands of LLM-mediated reasoning, measurement and revision actions. It autonomously reproduces a published transmission-matrix experiment on a non-original platform and converts an abstract coherence-order theory into experimental observables, providing, to our knowledge, the first observation of this class of coherence-order structure. More importantly, in an open-ended study involving 145.9 million tokens, 3,242 LLM calls, 1,242 tool calls, 163 research notes and 44 scripts, Qiushi Engine proposes and experimentally validates optical bilinear interaction, a physical mechanism structurally analogous to a core operation in Transformer attention. This AI-discovered mechanism suggests a route towards high-speed, energy-efficient optical hardware for pairwise computation. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an AI agentic system autonomously identifying and experimentally validating a nontrivial, previously unreported physical mechanism, marking a milestone for research-level autonomous agents.
speedrun: Researchers have developed the Qiushi Discovery Engine, an AI system that autonomously conducts scientific research on an optical platform. This system executed a complex experiment involving 145.9 million tokens and over 3,200 LLM calls, leading to the discovery of a new optical interaction mechanism. This achievement showcases the potential for AI to independently drive scientific discovery, which could change how research is conducted in the future.
why_it_matters: This could empower researchers by automating complex experiments, allowing them to focus on higher-level scientific questions.
The development signals a shift towards AI-driven research, potentially reducing costs and increasing efficiency in scientific discovery.

lenses.eli12: The Qiushi Discovery Engine is like having a super-smart assistant that can run experiments and learn from them all on its own. It completed a huge study and discovered a new way to understand light interactions. This matters because it shows that AI can help make real scientific advancements, which could lead to new technologies that benefit everyone.
lenses.pm: For product managers or founders, the Qiushi Discovery Engine represents a significant user need for automated research tools. This could reduce time and costs associated with traditional research methods. Companies might consider developing AI systems that can autonomously validate new ideas, streamlining innovation processes.
lenses.engineer: The Qiushi Engine employs a dual-layer architecture and Meta-Trace memory to maintain stable research trajectories. It executed 3,242 LLM calls and validated a new optical bilinear interaction, analogous to Transformer attention mechanisms. This demonstrates the potential of AI in discovering complex physical mechanisms, although practical applications may still require further exploration.
